title: Cat Vocalizing Pain When Picked Up: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat crying out when lifted under their arms? Discover potential causes like injuries or arthritis and learn when to seek veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat vocalizes when lifted, it indicates localized pain in the chest, ribs, or spine and requires a veterinary exam within 24-48 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if the vocalization is accompanied by labored breathing or extreme lethargy.

Why does my cat vocalize or cry out when I pick them up?

  • Vocalizing when picked up under the front legs is a clinical sign of localized discomfort or referred pain.
  • This reaction often indicates issues with the thoracic region, including the ribs, spine, or front limb joints.
  • Pain may also originate from the soft tissue and muscles in the chest area.
  • Because cats are masters at masking illness, an audible vocalization is a significant communication that something is physically wrong.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat cries when being handled?

  • The urgency level for this symptom is Medium; while not usually immediately life-threatening, it suggests an underlying condition requiring professional evaluation.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is also experiencing labored breathing, extreme lethargy, or blue-tinged gums.

How can sharing a photo or video help a veterinarian triage my cat?

  • Sharing a video of your cat’s posture or a photo of how they are resting allows a triage professional to observe natural behavior without the stress of a clinical environment.
  • Visual documentation allows a veterinarian to assess the cat's physical alignment and specific reactions to movement.
  • Seeing these behaviors helps narrow down whether the issue is skeletal, muscular, or related to an internal sensitivity.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a cat vocalizes in pain upon being picked up, potential causes include underlying painful conditions that should be evaluated during triage. Observable signs of pain may include vocalization, behavioral changes such as avoidance or aggression, and physical changes like tachycardia or dilated pupils. Diagnostic considerations should include examination for sources of hemorrhage, airway obstruction or disruption, breathing difficulties such as cyanosis, and circulatory issues. Further diagnostics may be necessary to rule out other causes of pain.

Chapter: Neurology, Dermatology, Psychiatry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Could my cat have a broken rib?
faqs.answer: Yes, rib fractures or bruising from a fall or accidental squeeze can cause sharp pain when pressure is applied to the chest area during lifting.

faqs.question: How can I safely move my cat if they are in pain?
faqs.answer: Avoid lifting them under the arms. Instead, slide one hand under their chest and use the other hand to fully support their hindquarters, keeping their spine level and supported.

faqs.question: Are there other signs of pain I should look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for changes in appetite, hiding more than usual, decreased grooming, or a 'hunched' sitting posture that differs from their normal relaxed state.
